summary: I was born in the Mae Kai Key refugee camp, which is located between the Thai and Burmese borders. My parents had fled from Burma and settled there before moving to another camp. Although I was born in that camp in 1998, I lived there only until I was four years old before we had to move due to a devastating flash flood.

Thailand is the country where the Mae Kai Key refugee camp and other camps like Ban Sala and Mae La Oon are located near the border with Burma. My family lived in these camps in Thailand as refugees after fleeing Burma. Thailand was a place of temporary refuge for us before we eventually resettled in the United States.

Burma is where my parents originally came from before fleeing to refugee camps in Thailand. Life there was difficult and dangerous, which forced my family to leave and seek safety across the border. Many of my relatives still live in Burma, and some chose not to leave because of family responsibilities or fear of starting a new life elsewhere.

My parents resettled in the Ban Sala refugee camp in 1998, which is where I was born. I remember that time as one filled with community activities like singing carols. We stayed there until the flash flood in 2002 forced us to move to another camp for safety.

After the flash flood in 2002 at Ban Sala, my family and I moved to the Mae La Oon camp. I was still very young and didn’t understand why we had to live in refugee camps. Over time, I learned about the hardships and limitations of camp life and why my parents had no choice but to stay there.

In 2013, my parents decided to move to the United States because many Karen people had resettled there and were living better lives. We went through a long process of interviews, tests, and screenings before arriving in November 2014. The United States offered us freedom, job opportunities, stable housing, and better education for me and my siblings.

When we arrived in the United States in November 2014, my family and I settled in St. Paul, Minnesota. We had some relatives here who came before us, which helped us adjust. Although it was scary at first because we didn’t speak English and had to leave behind friends and family, life in St. Paul has improved greatly and I now have dreams and goals I believe I can achieve.
